hinge: A vital tooth means the pulp is alive; a periapical radiolucency in a vital tooth must be of periodontal or non-odontogenic origin since a necrotic pulp is required for primary endodontic periapical pathosis. This fits primary perio origin.

For a true combined or primary endo-secondary perio lesion with deep pocket, abscess or cyst, the standard sequence is endodontic treatment first to eliminate the intraradicular infection source, then reassess periodontal healing. Surgical intervention is only if endo alone fails to resolve the lesion.

A broken file in the middle third of a canal that cannot be bypassed or retrieved carries a fair prognosis at best, since it blocks apical cleaning and obturation. Prognosis depends heavily on whether apical cleaning can still be achieved.

An asymptomatic tooth with two separated instruments, no periapical pathosis, and good obturation should ideally be observed. However, as no follow-up option is provided, the least invasive active option is attempting to bypass and retreat. This item is flawed without an observation option.

A sound tooth becoming necrotic in a patient with generalized horizontal bone loss indicates retrograde pulp necrosis from deep periodontal pockets tracking bacteria to the apex — primary perio with secondary endo involvement.
Mandibular central incisors most commonly have one canal, but when a second canal is present it is almost always located lingual to the main canal.
A crown fracture involving enamel and dentin without pulp exposure is classified as an uncomplicated crown fracture. Complicated fractures include pulp exposure.

A mid-root circular radiolucency in a tooth negative to cold could be internal or external resorption. Before initiating treatment, a shifted radiograph or CBCT is needed to differentiate between internal and external resorption, as management differs significantly.

Avulsion >60 minutes with an open apex has the worst prognosis because PDL is necrotic from prolonged extraoral time and the open apex eliminates revascularization potential, leading to inevitable ankylosis or resorption.
Smear layer removal opens dentinal tubules, allowing sealer penetration for improved micromechanical retention and a better apical seal against bacterial leakage.

In an elderly patient with calcified canals, cold testing may be negative despite a vital pulp, while EPT can still elicit a response through remaining neural elements. Normal percussion and palpation support a normal pulp and periapical diagnosis.
Direct pulp capping in primary teeth is contraindicated due to the high risk of internal resorption from chronic inflammation of the undifferentiated mesenchymal cells in the primary pulp.

For a traumatic pulp exposure within 24 hours with a pinpoint exposure, partial (Cvek) pulpotomy removes only the superficial inflamed pulp tissue and preserves the healthy radicular pulp, especially in young patients with open apices.

Glass ionomer cement provides the best coronal seal among temporary filling materials due to its chemical bonding to tooth structure, significantly reducing microleakage compared to Cavit, IRM, or Fermit.

A sound tooth that is necrotic with percussion pain suggests retrograde pulp involvement from a deep periodontal pocket reaching the apex — primary perio with secondary endo. However, the stem is abbreviated and lacks probing depth details.
Per the SLOB (Same Lingual, Opposite Buccal) rule: when the tube shifts mesially and the object moves mesially (follows the beam), the object is on the lingual side — therefore mesiolingual.
Pain on biting with large restorations on two adjacent teeth requires percussion testing first to localize which tooth is symptomatic before further pulp sensibility testing or imaging.
Removing the smear layer opens dentinal tubules and allows sealer penetration, improving the seal, reducing microleakage, and enhancing the adaptation of obturation materials to canal walls.

Short roots with bone replacement visible radiographically is external replacement resorption — the root is being replaced by bone (ankylosis). Surface resorption is shallow and self-limiting; cervical resorption is at the CEJ level.

A gap between gutta-percha and the metal post represents a coronal seal failure. Pain on biting indicates periapical inflammation. The appropriate management is to redo the RCT to achieve a proper seal throughout the canal system.
Maxillary first premolars typically have two canals (buccal and palatal), with approximately 85% having two canals and a minority having one or three.
Calcium hydroxide requires a minimum of 1-2 weeks in the canal to achieve its antimicrobial effect through sustained high pH release and diffusion into dentinal tubules.

High-resolution endodontic CBCT requires small voxel sizes. Among the listed options, 0.04mm (40μm) is the closest to the ultra-high-resolution settings used for detecting fine endodontic anatomy such as MB2 canals or root fractures, though 0.08mm is more commonly cited as the minimum for endodontic applications.

The H-file (Hedström) has a positive rake angle and cuts only on the withdrawal stroke, making it the most aggressive cutting instrument among hand files. K-files and reamers scrape rather than actively cut.

For a 13-year-old with an avulsed tooth stored in milk (physiologic medium), the tooth should be replanted directly and splinted. Extra-oral RCT would be for >60 min dry time with closed apex; fluoride treatment is for prolonged extraoral dry time. Without time or apex status, splinting directly is the most conservative first step assuming relatively short extraoral time with a suitable storage medium.

Non-vital (internal) bleaching with hydrogen peroxide or sodium perborate can diffuse through dentinal tubules to the external cervical root surface, initiating external cervical resorption. This is a well-documented complication.

CBCT is the most sensitive initial imaging modality for detecting vertical root fractures, as it provides three-dimensional visualization that overcomes the limitations of 2D periapical radiographs where the fracture line must be parallel to the x-ray beam to be visible.

A single side-vented needle is used for endodontic irrigation to prevent apical extrusion of sodium hypochlorite by providing a safety vent, which could cause severe complications in a tooth with prior trauma and possible periapical communication.

The first approach to a broken instrument is to attempt bypass or retrieval. Only if this is unsuccessful and the tooth is symptomatic should extraction or obturation to the level of the file be considered.

Profuse bleeding during pulpotomy that is difficult to control indicates the inflammation has extended beyond the coronal pulp into the radicular pulp — irreversible pulpitis. Healthy pulp tissue in a reversible pulpitis should produce controlled bleeding that stops within a few minutes.
